units.unit_name: Curricular Goals and Competencies - Matter, Physical World, Living World
topics: CG 1 – Explores the world of matter, its interactions, and properties at the atomic level

C 1.1 – Describes classification of elements in the Periodic Table, and explains how compounds (including carbon compounds) are formed based on the atomic structure (Bohr's model) and properties (valency).

C 1.2 – Investigates the nature and properties of chemical substances (distillation, crystallisation, chromatography, centrifugation, types and properties of mixtures, solutions, colloids, and suspensions)
C 1.3 – Describes and represents chemical interactions and changes using symbols and chemical equations (acid and base, metal and non-metal, reversible and irreversible)

units.unit_name: Curricular Goals and Competencies - Physical World Principles
topics: CG 2 – Explores the physical world around them, and understands scientific principles and laws based on observations and analysis

C 2.1 – Applies Newton's laws to explain the effect of forces (change in state of motion — displacement and direction, velocity and acceleration, uniform circular motion, acceleration due to gravity) and analyses graphical and mathematical representations of motion in one dimension
C 2.2 – Explains the relationship between mass and weight using universal law of gravitation, and connect it to the laws of motion

C 2.3 – Manipulates the position of object and properties of lenses (focus, centre of curvature) to observe image characteristics and correspondence with a ray diagram, and extends this understanding to a combination of lenses (telescope, microscope)

C 2.4 – Manipulates and analyses different characteristics of the circuit (current, voltage, resistance) and mathematises their relationship (Ohm's law), and applies it to everyday usage (electricity bill, short circuit, safety measures)
C 2.5 – Defines work in scientific terms, and represents the relationship between potential and kinetic energy (conservation of energy) in mathematical expressions
C 2.6 – Demonstrates the principle of mechanical advantage by constructing simple machines (system of levers and pulleys)
C 2.7 – Describes the origin and properties of sound (wavelength, frequency, amplitude) and differences in what we hear as it propagates through different instruments

C 2.8 – Explores interconnected systems and phenomena that support and affect life on Earth (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere and their interrelationships, earth processes, hazards, etc.)
key_concepts: Earth systems (hydrosphere, biosphere, atmosphere, geosphere, cryosphere)

sourceSessionPlan.title: Acids, Bases and Salts

teacherLessonNotes.sessionOverview: This session introduces Class X students to the fundamental properties of acids and bases. Students will explore historical context (Lavoisier), sensory characteristics (sour vs. bitter/soapy), natural occurrences in daily life, and the critical role of indicators like litmus, turmeric, and phenolphthalein in identifying acidic or basic nature.
prerequisiteKnowledge: Basic understanding of matter and its states
Familiarity with common household substances (lemon juice, soap, vinegar)
Concept that different substances have different properties
learningOutcomes: Students will be able to define acids and bases based on sensory characteristics.
Students will identify natural sources of common acids and bases.
Students will demonstrate the use of litmus paper as an indicator for acidic or basic solutions.
Students will explain how different indicators (turmeric, phenolphthalein) change color in response to pH changes.

teachingPlan.teachingStrategy: Sensory Hook: Tasting and smelling safe substances

lessonPurpose: To establish the foundational vocabulary of acids and bases.
To connect abstract chemical concepts to tangible daily experiences.
To introduce the scientific method of using indicators for identification.
teachingSequence: Begin with a 'Mystery Taste' activity (using safe, diluted substances) or visual prompts of sour vs. soapy items.
Introduce Antoine Lavoisier's naming convention: Acidus (sour), Base (alkali).
List properties on the board: Acids taste sour and turn blue litmus red; Bases taste bitter/soapy and turn red litmus blue.
Discuss natural sources: Citric acid in citrus, Lactic acid in curd, etc.
Demonstrate indicator tests using turmeric paste/stain and phenolphthalein solution on various household items (vinegar, soap water).
Explain the color change mechanism simply without deep chemical theory yet.
Conclude with a summary of how indicators act as 'chemical detectives'.
guidedPractice: Ask students to predict whether lemon juice or baking soda solution will turn red litmus blue, then verify.
Have students identify the color change when turmeric is added to soap water vs. vinegar.

lessonBlocks.title: Introduction: The Sour and Soapy World
teacherPrompt: Who has ever tasted a lemon? What did it taste like?
What happens when you touch soap with wet hands?
explanation: We classify substances based on their properties. Two major groups in chemistry are Acids and Bases.
The word 'Acid' comes from the Latin 'acidus', meaning sour (like Lavoisier named them).
Bases often feel soapy or bitter.
examples: Lemon juice, vinegar, curd are acidic. Soap, baking soda solution are basic.
Ant sting contains formic acid; nettle leaves have methanoic acid.
checkUnderstanding: Can anyone name a food item that is acidic?

lessonBlocks.title: Indicators: Chemical Detectives
teacherPrompt: How can we be sure a substance is an acid without tasting it?
What if the solution is colorless? How do we know?
explanation: We use 'Indicators'—substances that change color in acidic or basic solutions.
Litmus: Extracted from lichens. Blue litmus turns red in acid; Red litmus turns blue in base.
Turmeric: Yellow in neutral/acidic, turns reddish-brown in bases (like soap).
Phenolphthalein: Colorless in acidic/neutral, turns pink in basic solutions.
examples: Testing vinegar with blue litmus paper.
Adding phenolphthalein to a glass of water vs. soapy water.
Turmeric stain on a white shirt turning red after washing with soap.
boardWork: | Indicator | Acidic Solution | Basic Solution | Neutral |
| Litmus (Blue) -> Red | Blue litmus turns RED | No change / Stays Blue |
| Litmus (Red) -> Blue | No change / Stays Red | Red litmus turns BLUE |
boardSteps: Draw a table with columns: Indicator, Acidic Effect, Basic Effect.
Fill in Litmus changes first as the primary indicator.
checkUnderstanding: If I add phenolphthalein to a solution and it turns pink, is it acidic or basic?

conceptFlow.definition: Substances that taste sour and turn blue litmus red.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Acids are characterized by their ability to release hydrogen ions (H+) in water, though at this stage we focus on sensory properties. They react with metals and carbonates but primarily identified here by the 'sour' trait and indicator reactions.
conceptFlow.importance: Essential for understanding digestion, industrial processes, and environmental pH balance.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: To understand how our body digests food and how to treat insect bites safely.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Builds on the concept of matter properties introduced in earlier grades.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Leads directly to neutralization reactions, pH scale calculations, and salt formation.
visuals: Image of a lemon and an ant sting diagram.

conceptFlow.definition: Substances that taste bitter, feel soapy to touch, and turn red litmus blue.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Bases often release hydroxide ions (OH-) in water. They are the opposite of acids in terms of sensory properties but share reactivity with indicators.
conceptFlow.importance: Used extensively in cleaning products, soap making, and neutralizing acidic soils.
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: To understand hygiene, cleaning mechanisms, and soil treatment for agriculture.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Contrasts with the properties of acids learned in this session.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Critical for understanding neutralization (Acid + Base -> Salt + Water).
teacherMoves: Demonstrate the 'soapy' feel safely with a wet cloth.
visuals: Turmeric stain turning red on a white cloth.

conceptFlow.definition: Substances that change color in acidic or basic solutions to identify their nature.
conceptFlow.coreExplanation: Natural and synthetic indicators act as visual signals. Litmus is the most common natural indicator, while turmeric and phenolphthalein are specific for bases.
conceptFlow.importance: Allows scientists to classify unknown substances without tasting them (safety).
conceptFlow.whyStudyIt: To develop a safe method of chemical identification and understand pH sensitivity.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithPrevious: Extends the concept of 'properties' to include color-changing properties.
conceptFlow.relationshipWithFuture: Foundation for understanding Universal Indicator, pH scale (0-14), and titration.
teacherMoves: Show a bottle of phenolphthalein solution.
examples: Blue litmus paper turning red in vinegar.
Yellow turmeric paste turning reddish-brown when mixed with soap water.
visuals: Diagram showing the color transition of phenolphthalein (Colorless -> Pink).

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: All bases are alkalis, and all acids taste sour so they must be safe to eat.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Clarify that while many common acids (citric) are edible in dilute forms, laboratory acids can be dangerous. Also, clarify the distinction between 'base' (chemical class) and 'alkali' (soluble base).

commonMisconceptionsDetailed.misconception: Phenolphthalein turns red in acidic solutions.
commonMisconceptionsDetailed.correction: Reiterate that Phenolphthalein is colorless in acid/neutral and pink only in basic solutions.

Provide a printed chart of indicators with colors for reference during activities.
averageLearners: Encourage them to list 3 household items that are acidic and 3 that are basic on their own.
advancedLearners: Ask: 'Why does turmeric not change color in acid? What chemical property allows it to detect bases specifically?' (Introductory thought).

teacherTips: Always emphasize safety: Never taste chemicals in a lab setting.
Use the term 'Indicator' frequently as a metaphor for a detective or signal light.
misconceptions: Students may think all sour things are acids and vice versa without understanding chemical definitions.
Confusion between natural indicators (turmeric) and synthetic ones (phenolphthalein).
formativeChecks: Observe students during the demonstration of turmeric color change.
assessmentQuestions: Ask a student to identify if vinegar is acidic or basic based on its smell/taste description and predict litmus result.
Quick fire: 'Blue litmus in acid? Red litmus in base?'
What happens to turmeric when soap water is added?

sourceSessionPlan.introduction: Imagine you have a bottle of clear liquid. Is it water? Vinegar? Or soap solution? You can't tell just by looking! Today, we become chemical detectives using our senses (safely) and special 'color-changing' substances called indicators to solve the mystery of Acids and Bases.

theory.overview: Acids and bases are two fundamental classes of chemicals with opposite properties. Acids taste sour and turn blue litmus red, while bases taste bitter/soapy and turn red litmus blue. We use indicators like turmeric and phenolphthalein to identify them visually.
keyPoints: Acids: Sour (e.g., Lemon), Blue Litmus -> Red
theory.detailedContent: The study of acids and bases began with Antoine Lavoisier who named them based on taste. 'Acid' comes from the Latin word 'acidus' meaning sour, while 'Base' refers to substances that are alkaline or soapy. Common examples include citric acid in lemons and acetic acid in vinegar for acids; baking soda and soap solutions for bases. To identify these without tasting (which is dangerous), we use indicators. Litmus paper turns red in acidic solutions and blue in basic ones. Turmeric, a natural indicator found in spices, stays yellow in neutral/acidic conditions but turns reddish-brown when it touches a base like soap water. Phenolphthalein is another common synthetic indicator that remains colorless in acids but turns bright pink in bases.

activities.name: The Color-Changing Detective
instructions: Teacher prepares three small beakers: one with vinegar (acid), one with soap water (base), and one with plain water (neutral).
Distribute blue litmus paper, red litmus paper, turmeric paste on a slide, and phenolphthalein solution to groups.
Students dip the papers into each beaker or add drops of indicator to observe color changes.
Record observations in a table: Substance | Indicator Used | Color Change | Nature (Acid/Base/Neutral).
Discuss why turmeric is useful for detecting soap but not vinegar.

slides.teachingBeat: Explain the chemical basis of acidity (H+) and basicity (OH-) through ionization.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Why Do They Conduct Electricity?
> Acid Dissociation:
> HA -> H+ + A-
- BOH -> B+ + OH-
- Dissolving in Water -> Ion Formation
- - Acid: Releases Hydrogen Ions (H+)
![Why Do They Conduct Electricity?](http://localhost:3002/api/ppt-assets/558b98f6b01ca32211c6c210b0bd4110aad569c0.svg)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Explain the chemical basis of acidity (H+) and basicity (OH-) through ionization.
Purpose: Introduce the Arrhenius concept.
Explain: When acids dissolve in water, they split into ions. The H+ ion is responsible for acidic properties.
Ask: Why does pure distilled water not conduct electricity?
Listen for: No ions present.
Correct: Exactly! Tap water conducts because of dissolved salts/ions.
Board work: Write the dissociation equations clearly on the board. Highlight H+ and OH- in different colors.
-->
